steps.text: Give an initial briefing at the site to personnel prior to their participation in any emergency response.
Ensure specialist employees receive training or demonstrate competency in their area of specialization annually.
Convey required skill and knowledge levels to all new responders through training.
Train employees who participate, or are expected to participate, in emergency response in accordance with the specified paragraphs.
Ensure first responders at the awareness level have sufficient training or experience to objectively demonstrate competency in understanding hazardous substances and their risks.

preconditions.text: Prior to participation in any emergency response
Before new responders are permitted to take part in actual emergency operations on an incident

constraints.text: The initial briefing shall include instruction in the wearing of appropriate personal protective equipment.
The initial briefing shall include instruction in what chemical hazards are involved.
All other appropriate safety and health precautions provided to the employer's own employees shall be used.

steps.text: Review and discuss the site safety plan as part of the training program.
Discuss the location of the site safety plan and all written programs, including mechanisms for access, review, and references.
Review the applicable paragraphs of 29 CFR 1910.120 and the elements of the employer’s occupational safety and health plan.
Review relevant hazards such as chemical, biological, and radiological exposures, fire and explosion hazards, thermal extremes, and physical hazards.
Review general safety hazards including electrical, powered equipment, lockout/tagout, motor vehicle, and walking-working surface hazards.
Review confined space hazards and procedures.
Review work practices to minimize employee risk from workplace hazards.
Review emergency response plan and procedures including first aid meeting the requirements of paragraph (p)(8).
Review procedures to minimize exposure to hazardous waste and various types of waste streams, including the materials handling program and spill containment program.
Review hazard communication programs meeting the requirements of 29 CFR 1910.1200.
Review medical surveillance programs meeting the requirements of 29 CFR 1910.120(p)(3) including the recognition of signs and symptoms of overexposure.
Review decontamination programs and procedures meeting the requirements of 29 CFR 1910.120(p)(4).
Review an employer’s requirements to implement a training program.
Conduct training of the classroom or on-the-job type.
Establish employee proficiency in the work practices and introduce compliance procedures.
Certify that each employee has received the required training.
Maintain the certification for the duration of the employee's employment.
Provide medical services and first aid as required in Section 1910.151.
Ensure persons trained in first-aid including CPR are available.

preconditions.text: Employee demonstrates proficiency in the work practices involved
Employees are performing work on or associated with exposed lines or equipment energized at 50 volts or more

constraints.text: For field work involving two or more employees at a work location, at least two trained persons shall be available (or only one if all new employees are trained in first aid, including CPR, within 3 months of hiring).

For fixed work locations, the number of trained persons available shall be sufficient to ensure that each employee exposed to electric shock can be reached within 4 minutes (or all employees trained if existing number is insufficient).

steps.text: Allow employees to work with HIV or HBV.
Provide a training program to employees who have no prior experience in handling human pathogens.
Assign a progression of work activities as techniques are learned and proficiency is developed.
Maintain training records containing dates, contents, names/qualifications of trainers, and names/job titles of attendees.
Make records available upon request to the Assistant Secretary and the Director for examination and copying.
Provide employee training records upon request for examination and copying to employees and employee representatives.

preconditions.text: Employees must demonstrate proficiency in standard microbiological practices and techniques and in the practices and operations specific to the facility.
Employees must have prior experience in the handling of human pathogens or tissue cultures.
Employees participate in work activities involving infectious agents only after proficiency has been demonstrated.

constraints.text: Initial work activities shall not include the handling of infectious agents.
Training records shall be maintained for 3 years from the date on which the training occurred.

steps.text: Provide employees with information and training to ensure they are apprised of the hazards of chemicals present in their work area.
Determine the frequency of refresher information and training.
Inform employees of the standard's contents, the Chemical Hygiene Plan, permissible exposure limits, signs and symptoms of chemical exposure, and availability of reference materials.
Train employees on hazard detection methods, physical and health hazards of chemicals, and protective measures (including work practices, emergency procedures, and PPE).

preconditions.text: Employee is given an initial assignment to a work area where hazardous chemicals are present.
Employee is given an assignment involving new exposure situations.

steps.text: Initiate and maintain accident prevention programs as may be necessary to comply with this part.
Provide for frequent and regular inspections of the job sites, materials, and equipment to be made by competent persons designated by the employers.
Permit only those employees qualified by training or experience to operate equipment and machinery.
Provide training on hazards and related matters as required by the standards.

constraints.text: Competent persons must be capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and have authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

A qualified employee is one who, by possession of a recognized degree, certificate, or professional standing, or who by extensive knowledge, training, and experience, has successfully demonstrated his ability to solve or resolve problems relating to the subject matter, the work, or the project.

steps.text: Orient operating personnel to changes in procedures before the change is made.
Update operating procedures before startup of the process.
Train personnel in handling upset conditions and emergency procedures.
Maintain communication between operating personnel and workers performing work within the process area.
Convey the hazards of the tasks to operating personnel and to those performing the actual tasks.
Inform operating personnel when the work is completed to provide closure on the job.
Clearly define the employees to be trained and what subjects are to be covered in their training.

preconditions.text: When the process is shut down in order to make a change

constraints.text: All employees, including maintenance and contractor employees, involved with highly hazardous chemicals must fully understand the safety and health hazards of the chemicals and processes they work with.
Training must be conducted in compliance with 1926.59, the Hazard Communication standard, and cover additional subjects such as operating procedures, safety work practices, and emergency response.

steps.text: Certify that each covered employee has attended and successfully completed the required training, or certify the employee's competency at least yearly.
Record and maintain the method used to demonstrate competency.
Rehearse the emergency response plan regularly as part of the overall training program for site operations.
Ensure specialist employees receive training or demonstrate competency in their area of specialization annually.

constraints.text: Training shall be based on the duties and function to be performed by each responder of an emergency response organization.

steps.text: Consider the topics listed in 1926.65(q)(6) when developing response training programs.
Supplement training programs with hazard recognition, including the nature of hazardous substances and practical applications (biology, chemistry, and physics).
Supplement training programs with principles of toxicology, biological monitoring, and risk assessment.
Supplement training programs with safe work practices and general site safety.
Supplement training programs with engineering controls.

preconditions.text: Interact with the individuals responsible for the site where the response would be expected.

steps.text: Maintain proper warning signs where such a circuit exists.
Advise employees of the location of such lines, the hazards involved, and the protective measures to be taken.
Erect, move, dismantle, or alter scaffolds.
Train each employee who performs work while on a scaffold to recognize hazards and understand control procedures.

preconditions.text: The scaffold activities must be under the supervision and direction of a competent person qualified in scaffold erection, moving, dismantling, or alteration.
The scaffold activities must be performed only by experienced and trained employees selected for such work by the competent person.

constraints.text: Qualified means one who, by possession of a recognized degree, certificate, or professional standing, or who by extensive knowledge, training, and experience, has successfully demonstrated his/her ability to solve or resolve problems related to the subject matter, the work, or the project.

The training must include: electrical, fall, and falling object hazards; correct procedures for dealing with hazards and safety systems; proper use of the scaffold and material handling; and maximum intended load and capacities.

steps.text: Provide a training program for each employee who might be exposed to fall hazards to enable them to recognize hazards and minimize them.
Assure that each employee has been trained, as necessary, by a competent person qualified in the required fall protection areas.
Verify compliance with training requirements by preparing a written certification record.

preconditions.text: Employee might be exposed to fall hazards

constraints.text: The written certification record shall contain the name or other identity of the employee trained, the date(s) of the training, and the signature of the person who conducted the training or the signature of the employer.

steps.text: A competent person shall evaluate the work area, the projected work practices, and the engineering controls.

Certify in writing that the different or modified controls are adequate to reduce direct and indirect employee exposure to below the PELs under all expected conditions of use and that the method meets the requirements of this standard.
Conduct an inspection of the worksite and determine that the roofing material is intact and will likely remain intact.

preconditions.text: Before work begins and as needed during the job

constraints.text: All employees performing work covered by paragraph (g)(11) shall be trained in a training program that meets the requirements of paragraph (k)(9)(viii).

steps.text: Conduct inspections using inspectors qualified to recognize and evaluate hazards of the working environment and to suggest general abatement procedures.
Provide all inspection personnel with the equipment necessary to conduct a thorough inspection of the workplace involved.
Operate and maintain listings in the 'Federal Supply Schedule' of safety and health services and equipment approved for use by agencies.

preconditions.text: Inspectors must have experience and/or up-to-date training in occupational safety and health hazard recognition and evaluation (or sufficient documented training and/or experience for less complex hazards).

constraints.text: Each agency must utilize as inspectors personnel with equipment and competence to recognize hazards.

steps.text: Call 1-800-321-OSHA (6742) or go to www.osha.gov to find the contact information for the OSHA Federal or state plan office nearest you.
